food_subgroup: Grilled fish set meal with rice and side dishes

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn does not have a specific entry for hotplate saba set meal. Closest parent is Atlantic mackerel food product (FOODON_03301116); the full set meal context including rice and sides is not captured.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers cooked mackerel as a closest parent. The hotplate preparation method and set-meal composition (rice, sides) are not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for hotplate saba set meal as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hotplate saba set meal consisting of approximately 150 g grilled saba fillet, 200 g steamed white rice, miso soup, and pickled vegetable sides, totalling approximately 450 g.

nutrient_source.title: Mackerel, Atlantic, cooked, dry heat — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 175097); Cooked white rice — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 169756); composite set-meal estimate weighted by component proportions
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for hotplate saba set meal exists in any primary regional database. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite: approximately 33% grilled Atlantic mackerel (USDA SR Legacy FDC 175097), 44% cooked white rice (USDA SR Legacy FDC 169756), and 23% miso soup and pickled vegetable sides (USDA SR Legacy FDC 172459 for miso soup). Soy-based glaze sodium contribution estimated from standard teriyaki glaze data. Values represent per-100 g of the full set meal as served.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Foster-Powell et al.; white rice (boiled, Japanese-style) GI ~54–56; mackerel and fish components contribute negligible GI
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hotplate saba set meal. GI of 54 is estimated from the dominant glycemic component: Japanese-style boiled white rice (GI ~54, University of Sydney GI Database). The saba fish fillet and miso soup contribute negligible glycemic load. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (54 × 78.75) / 100 ≈ 42.5, where available carbohydrate per 450 g serving = 17.5 × 4.5 = 78.75 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy-based glaze and miso soup
hypertension.guidance: The hotplate saba set meal contains approximately 310 mg sodium per 100 g (approximately 1,395 mg per full serving), primarily from the soy-based glaze and miso soup; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-sodium glaze and limit or omit miso soup to keep total meal sodium within the recommended 600–800 mg per meal threshold.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load from white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The set meal has a low glycemic index of approximately 54 driven by the white rice component, with a glycemic load of approximately 42.5 per serving; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider requesting a smaller rice portion or substituting with brown rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion, while the saba fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that support insulin sensitivity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Omega-3 fatty acids from saba mackerel
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saba (Atlantic mackerel) is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), with an estimated 1.5 g polyunsaturated fat per 100 g of the set meal; regular consumption of oily fish such as saba is associated with reduced triglycerides and cardiovascular risk, though the sodium content from the glaze and miso should be monitored in patients with concurrent hypertension.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hotplate Saba Set (铁板鲭鱼套餐) is a Japanese-style set meal featuring a grilled saba (Atlantic mackerel) fillet cooked on a sizzling iron hotplate, served with steamed white rice, miso soup, and pickled vegetable sides. The saba fillet is typically marinated or glazed with a soy-based sauce, then grilled until the skin is crispy and the flesh is tender and flaky, with a savory-sweet umami flavour profile. The hotplate presentation keeps the fish hot and aromatic at the table. Saba is one of the most nutritionally dense oily fish consumed in Japanese cuisine, providing high-quality protein (approximately 9.2 g per 100 g of the set meal), omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12 (approximately 3.5 mcg per 100 g), vitamin D (approximately 2.1 mcg per 100 g), and selenium. The white rice component contributes the majority of the carbohydrate content (approximately 17.5 g per 100 g), giving the meal a low glycemic index of approximately 54. The soy-based glaze and miso soup contribute significant sodium (approximately 310 mg per 100 g, or approximately 1,395 mg per full serving), which is a clinical consideration for individuals with hypertension. In Singapore, this dish is served at Japanese and Korean-fusion restaurants and is popular as a balanced, protein-rich lunch or dinner option. Clinically, the omega-3 content supports cardiovascular health, while the moderate glycemic load requires portion awareness for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a saba set meal?
Is saba fish good for diabetes?
Is grilled mackerel high in sodium?
Is saba set meal suitable for heart patients?
What does hotplate saba taste like?

confidence_reason: No direct entry for hotplate saba set meal exists in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of USDA SR Legacy entries for grilled Atlantic mackerel, cooked white rice, and miso soup. GI is estimated from the dominant glycemic component (Japanese-style white rice) using the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is medium because the component-level sources are approved and defensible, but the composite weighting introduces estimation uncertainty.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates based on USDA SR Legacy data for individual components (grilled Atlantic mackerel FDC 175097, cooked white rice FDC 169756, miso soup FDC 172459) weighted by approximate serving proportions (33% fish, 44% rice, 23% soup and sides). 2. trans_fat_g is set to 0.0 as grilled fish and plain rice contain no industrially produced trans fats; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central component data. 3. Sodium estimate includes contribution from soy-based glaze (approximately 80 mg per 100 g of set meal) in addition to miso soup sodium; actual restaurant sodium may vary significantly. 4. GI of 54 is an analogous estimate based on Japanese-style boiled white rice from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found. 5. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and carry moderate uncertainty. 6. The input record lists the serving restaurant as 'Korean Cuisine - Funan Mall' despite the cuisine being tagged as Japanese; this dish appears to be served in a Korean-Japanese fusion context in Singapore. 7. Halal status is not confirmed as soy-based glaze formulation and kitchen practices at the specific restaurant are unknown. 8. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301116 is used as closest parent for Atlantic mackerel food product; no composite set-meal FoodOn code exists. 9. FoodEx2 code A00LC (mackerel, cooked) is used as closest parent; the full set-meal composition is not individually coded.
